components: στέργω / στοργή (stergō / storgē, to cherish, the attachment of kin)
root.note: φιλόστοργος is a parasýntheton (derived-compound) on φιλόστοργος per Etymologicum Magnum φ152, not a fresh compound of φίλος+στοργή.

meta.working_gloss: the built-in cherishing of one's own, above all of one's offspring: an attachment that arises κατὰ φύσιν (kata physin) rather than by decision and holds through hardship
meta.query: Pure word-search of the φιλοστοργ- family (noun φιλοστοργία, adjective φιλόστοργος, adverb φιλοστόργως, verb φιλοστοργέω, and the alpha-privative ἀφιλόστοργος) across the WHOLE corpus, no period/domain/author/affiliation filter, per the diachronic-term-survey method.
meta.scope: eulogikon corpus, all periods and affiliations. Stoic attestations are flagged (affiliation='stoic') as the pivot of the survey.
meta.method: Lemma tool (form_to_lemma + term_distribution): paradigm from eulogikon.lemma_forms/form_lemma matched into eulogikon.units via eulogikon.grc_fold() (diacritic-insensitive), joined works->authors. Tagged after collection with period/domain/dialect/affiliation/floruit from the DB. See guides track-a-term, track-term-over-time, semantic-drift, tekmeria and skill diachronic-term-survey.
meta.density_reading: Raw counts are for reading in context only, never a weighting input. Normalised by per-period surviving-character volume, attestation density peaks in the ROMAN imperial period (~2.0 / million chars), driven by Plutarch, Josephus, Epictetus and Galen; the archaic count is small in absolute terms but dense against its tiny surviving volume.
notes: lexical_status flags sense, not morphology: 'concept' = the affection word-family; 'privative_antonym' = ἀφιλόστοργος (its negation, Epictetus Diss. 2.17); 'proper_name'/'likely_proper_name' = personal names (Φιλόστοργος, Φιλοστόργιος) excluded from the concept counts.
The adverb φιλοστόργως is lexicalised under the adjective lemma φιλόστοργος.

Per the survey method, ecclesiastical/theological (Christian) and Byzantine material is retained in the data, flagged by affiliation/period, but is NOT built into the diachronic reading; the New Testament ἀφιλόστοργος (Rom 1:31, 2 Tim 3:3) is a parallel strand only.
No author is weighted or capped: a pattern's strength is the number of independent periods/domains/authors attesting it, not its token count.
meta.blacklist_compliance: Glosses checked live against eugraphikon.signal_triggers blacklist_* rows; φύσις/physis kept untranslated (never 'nature'/'natural'); no 'soul', 'essence', 'substance', 'will', 'fate', 'providence'.

paradigm.gloss: the cherishing-attachment itself, as a standing disposition
disposed to cherish one's own; (comparative -ότερος, superlative -ότατος)
to cherish, hold in tender attachment

collocational_clusters.gloss: the cherishing directed downward at one's offspring
collocational_clusters.spread: archaic (Theano) -> classical (Plato, Aristotle of animals) -> hellenistic Stoa (Chrysippus, Antipater) -> roman (Epictetus). Glossed φιλόστοργος = φιλότεκνος by Hesychius.

collocational_clusters.gloss: the cherishing as arising κατὰ φύσιν, an attachment a creature grows into rather than chooses
collocational_clusters.spread: classical (Xenophon φύσει φιλόστοργος; Aristotle φύσει φιλόστοργον) -> Stoa (Chrysippus φυσικήν; Epictetus κατὰ φύσιν / φυσική). The Stoic pivot.

collocational_clusters.gloss: the cherishing set beside the other kin-and-fellow attachments in one list
collocational_clusters.spread: hellenistic (Chrysippus lists it with φιλία, φιλοξενία, φιλανθρωπία; Antipater with εὔνοια; Posidonius with εὐσέβεια) -> roman (Marcus with σεμνότης, ἐλευθερία, δικαιότης).

collocational_clusters.gloss: the excess of cherishing that misleads and enslaves; the disposition put in question
collocational_clusters.spread: hellenistic (Antipater διὰ τὴν ἄγαν φιλοστοργίαν) -> roman (Epictetus 3.24 interrogates it: 'what kind of φιλοστοργία?' one that makes you a slave is not good).

collocational_clusters.gloss: the cherishing widened from household to homeland, benefactors and rulers
collocational_clusters.spread: hellenistic->roman historiography (Posidonius toward the nurturing homeland; Diodorus of benefactors; Josephus dynastic/Herod; Galen of devotion unto death).

collocational_clusters.gloss: to be disposed cherishingly toward someone; the adverbial manner
collocational_clusters.spread: archaic (Heraclitus the Allegorist) -> roman (Epictetus, Marcus φιλοστόργως καὶ ἀδήκτως).

collocational_clusters.gloss: the one in whom the built-in cherishing is absent
collocational_clusters.spread: roman (Epictetus 2.17). Parallel strand, not developed: NT Rom 1:31 / 2 Tim 3:3.

crossing_phrase.greek: ἡ κατὰ φύσιν στέρξις πρὸς τοὺς οἰκείους, μάλιστα πρὸς τὰ τέκνα, ἣ οὐκ ἐκ προαιρέσεως γίνεται ἀλλὰ συναύξεται, καὶ διαμένει διὰ τῶν χαλεπῶν
crossing_phrase.transliteration: hē kata physin sterxis pros tous oikeious, malista pros ta tekna, hē ouk ek prohaireseōs ginetai alla synauxetai, kai diamenei dia tōn chalepōn
crossing_phrase.gloss: the cherishing-attachment toward one's own, above all toward one's offspring, that does not come by decision but grows up together with the creature, and stays fast through hardship
crossing_phrase.coverage: Covers archaic through late-antique across philosophy, history, oratory, epic, medicine and grammar (>=6 domains, >=4 periods).
crossing_phrase.stoic_branch: The Stoa keeps this one attachment while purging the πάθη: Epictetus argues τὸ φιλόστοργον is κατὰ φύσιν and must be εὐλόγιστον (reasonable), not opposed to it; Marcus holds a person can be at once ἀπαθέστατον and φιλοστοργότατον. Epictetus 3.24 marks the branch where excess (ἄγαν) turns it into enslaving προσπάθεια.

status: PROVISIONAL — crossing holds across >=6 domains and >=4 periods, but embedding/vector coverage is partial (~14.6% pre-clean-text at last check); re-verify with term_distribution/search_text before promoting to CONTROLLED.

authors.era: 5th–6th c. CE (compilation); based on earlier material from late antiquity
